content: Hi :-) I didn't want to put this on the mailing lists because maybe it's a stupid idea and it's not something I'm able to implement myself. There was some talk about how to best utilize Rust's benefits. I was just wondering if maybe filesystems wouldn't be a good fit for a real driver -- they're "leaf" drivers, but interact very directly with users; they are independent of hardware; and correctness and safety guarantees is something that everybody wants from their filesystem. Maybe it would even be possible to write the code in such a way that you could compile it for userspace too and run unit tests against it without having to build and run a full kernel? (Which would make it infinitely more hackable than any current kernel filesystem implementation...)
Oh, and there's also #Modules > FS :rolling_eyes: seems I have some reading up to do.
For tests, my plan is to have them running within the kernel easily. Running things in in a VM or even in QEMU is very fast and you can iterate within a few seconds (plus test time).

For filesystems, while it is true that you could run some tests in userspace, I would imagine the complex/interesting tests around reliability, stress-testing, etc. would need to be run as a userspace + kernel combo anyway.

Hi Vegard! I'm the main grad student behind Bento! Feel free to ask me more about it. Some people are also working on writing RamFS in Rust in this repo. I think Fox Chen is leading that work. If you want to see more, the Github issue is here: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/369
